comments.title: Reincarnation
comments.content:
Hello JMG

I've been feeling a strong pull to reengage with christianity, RC with its colorful pantheon of saints or orthodoxy which seems to radiate an otherworldly golden light. My beliefs are really more on the open to polytheism, druidy nature mystic side of things and your essay on reincarnation nicely summed up what I have intuitivly felt to be true for a long time. Maybe I have an emotional need for some kind of certainty, community, ceremony or shelter in an edifice of ancient imagery and symbolism.

My question is: how did the revival druids of the 19th century reconcile their Anglican faith with the teachings of the Barddas? (especially those on reincarnation, which all major christian churches seem to reject categorically).
How did Dion Fortune manage to recite the Creed along with the rest of the congregation at mass and then go and write about what happens beyond the gates of death from the point of view of occult philosophy?
To really hammer out the principle I suppose one could also ask, could a contemporary heathen believe in reincarnation without breaking trust with the Asir?

Thank you for your generosity.

Mike

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
I am so glad you are asking these questions. This is a topic close to my heart and one that has been much on my mind lately. The kind of pull to re-engagement with sacramental Christianity in its color and grit is exactly what led me to ask a similar question last week.

But, like you, I also have been wondering about how others in the past have handled such reconciliation or if there's less of a divide as it seems. And, truthfully, the communal reciting of the Creed was the thing that led my wife and I to leave the church (she turned to me one Sunday during the recitation and said, "I just don't believe this anymore."). I wonder if there's not an esoteric interpretation that would allow one to engage with it in an authentic manner.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
The way that Dion Fortune dealt with the Creed et al. was the same way that most Christian occultists have done so. They remember that every religious teaching has an outward meaning suitable for the masses, and an inner meaning suitable for those who are prepared to take up spiritual practice. Just as Jesus taught the crowds with parables and reserved his inner teachings to the apostles, and as Paul spoke of giving milk to babes and meat to strong men, they hold that the Creed is a parable, and its real meaning is symbolic and needs to be understood through meditation and prayer. Thus when the Creed speaks of "the resurrection of the body, and the life everlasting," they held that this doesn't have to be taken in a simple-minded sort of literal sense; what is reincarnation, after all, but a resurrection in bodily form, and the soul that passes from life to life does in fact have life everlasting. All other aspects of the Creed, they held, can be understood in the same sort of symbolic sense.

As far as the Aesir, hmm. Where did you get the idea that the Aesir care what opinions you hold about the afterlife? That's purely a habit of prophetic religions...

comments.title: Smoking
comments.content: Do you know of any particular working that helps with breaking addictions, in my case, primarily smoking.

replies.title: Re: Smoking
replies.content:
This is a common question, and it's also a good way to get a better sense of the magical concept of the will.

You smoke because you choose to smoke. The habit of smoking isn't something outside you that holds you down and makes you light up; it's something you do, and something you choose to do. In my experience, most people who say they want to quit smoking don't actually want that; they think they ought to want that, or they've convinced themselves that they really should want that, but when it comes right down to it, they want to smoke, and that's why they keep smoking.

Christian moral theology has a lot to answer for. That's where people in our culture get the idea that morality is about dividing yourself in half, one half the demanding master, the other half the sullen slave. The master gives orders and the slave's supposed to obey them, but of course the slave inevitably cheats, or tempts, or goes through some other rigmarole to get his way. So you have this constant state of futile inner guerrilla warfare, where the master-self constantly tries and fails to impose his will on the slave-self, who just as constantly wriggles out of control. In this condition you can't will anything with a whole heart, and so your capacity to work effective magic is profoundly weakened.

Magical power comes from unity of will. When you can will one thing with all your heart, without quibbles or regrets, you can accomplish that thing if it's within the reach of a created being. My advice to smokers, then, and to anyone else who has a habit that's officially disapproved of but doesn't violate the laws or harm anyone else, is to ditch the pretense of being two different people, one yelling at the other to quit while the other lights up. There's just one of you involved, and if you want to smoke, do it. Be considerate about it around others, of course, but don't go skulking around with a hangdog look pretending to yourself that you don't want what you do in fact want.

And if you actually don't want to smoke, you'll stop. It really is that simple.

One other thing. Your health is not a moral issue. If you choose to have a habit that the current round of experts currently consider unhealthy, and you accept the consequences, that's nobody's business but yours. This notion that we all ought to heap shame and blame and moral panic onto yet another set of activities that harms nobody but the person who chooses to engage in them -- dear gods, haven't we already seen enough pointless misery from that habit already?

replies.title: Re: books on divination
replies.content: As far as I know there aren't any, because those kinds of divination aren't seriously practiced in traditional magic. What happened was that some years ago, certain people read about geomancy (the name of which literally means "earth divination") and jumped to the conclusion that there ought to be three more forms of divination using fire, air, and water. Geomancy isn't actually earth divination; the name was based on a misunderstanding of the Arabic term 'ilm ar-raml, "the science of the sand," so called because Arabic geomancers traditionally used a smooth patch of sand as the working surface for casting their charts. (In the Middle East, sand is the cheapest and most readily available writing surface!) So aeromancy and hydromancy are phantom methods; they show up in fantasy fiction fairly often, and also in some books, but as far as I know nobody does them in any serious way and there are no books about them.

replies.title: Re: James Blish and Black Easter/Day After Judgment (Devil's Day)
replies.content:
I've read Black Easter and its sequels -- you might include A Case of Conscience in that, though it's not so much a sequel as another work dealing with the same themes. I'm far from sure that he was any kind of an occultist; his rituals are patched together out of generally available materials -- the writings of A.E. Waite in particular -- and the plot line doesn't correspond to any version of occult philosophy I know of. It does make for good fiction, though.

A lot of people in the SF community in the mid-20th century dabbled in occultism, and if you know your occult traditions you can catch the references to various teachings and rituals in an assortment of classic SF. I don't know of any important SF writers who were serious occultists, though. In fantasy fiction, L. Frank Baum, Talbot Mundy, and H. Warner Munn were serious occultists. SF editors were the ones who tended to get deep into occultism -- Ray Palmer and John W. Campbell were both heavily into various branches of occultism.

replies.title: Re: James Blish and Black Easter/Day After Judgment (Devil's Day)
replies.content:
My first occult teacher, outside of my own family, was my high-school physics teacher, a man named Clesson Hopkins Harvey, who had grown up in the Point Loma community of Katherine Tingley's branch of the Theosophical Society, and then went on to take a BS degree in Physical Chemistry at UC Berkeley. He was rather fond of the occult fantasy writer, A. Merritt, along with Talbot Mundy. (Mundy was also a Point-Loma Theosophist of the generation of Harvey's parents.) Harvey could read Old Egyptian and Classical Tibetan, and drew much of his own take on occult matters from a detailed study of the Pyramid Texts and from the books on Tibetan esotericism by Walter Evans-Wentz. He had [what he considered to be] reliable inner-plane contacts.

As for better-known SF writers, Robert A. Heinlein's second wife, Leslyn MacDonald self-identified as a Witch and practiced magic; Heinlein presumably put some elements of her practice into his novel, "Magic, Inc.," and Amanda in that novel was probably modeled on her.

Fritz Leiber's first wife, Jonquil Stephens, was fascinated by Witchcraft all her life long, and she is clearly the inspiration for Tansy Saylor in "Conjure Wife"; Leiber himself was a life-long student of magic and occultism, but from a skeptical point of view.

Shirley Jackson belongs here, too, though she is not precisely a fantasy writer in the usual sense of the term. She began her study of magic during her college years, read avidly in excellent old books on magic ("books by dead people") all her life long, and practiced magic constantly, perhaps obsessively, in her own home. She also self-identified as a Witch, though somewhat with her tongue in her cheek.

replies.title: Re: James Blish and Black Easter/Day After Judgment (Devil's Day)
replies.content:
I forgot to mention Marion Zimmer Bradley, who had perhaps the deepest knowledge of magic of any 20th-century SF writer. As early as the last years of the '60s she was publishing high-quality regular advice columns on magic and occultism in the East Village Other (an alternative newspaper in New York City)under the pseudonym of Elfrida Rivers. By then she was already well aware of W. E. Butler's work, and recommended his books with some frequency. Moreover, her Darkover novels feature a form of magic under the guise of "matrix technology," on the assumption that magic is a subset of parapsychology.

In the early '90s, on (IIRC) the alt.magick listserv, there was a Witch whose coven was explicitly modeled on the Matrix Circles of Darkover, with a Keeper instead of a High Priestess.

replies.title: Re: James Blish and Black Easter/Day After Judgment (Devil's Day)
replies.content:
Several of the founders and early leaders of the pagan/witchcraft community of the San Francisco Bay Area, which got its underway in the mid-Sixties, were members of or had ties to the local community of science fiction and fantasy writers. Alison Harlow, who was one of the founders of the Society for Creative Anachronism and later a founder and First Officer of the Covenant of the Goddess, an organization of witches and covens, was married to Randall Garrett, a fantasy and sf writer. Fritz Leiber had friends among the initiates of the New Reformed Orthodox Order of the Golden Dawn, a witchcraft tradition founded in San Francisco in 1967 (NROOGD claims no connection with the original Golden Dawn, and usually goes by its initials to avoid confusing people.).

In the late Sixties and early Seventies, the overlap in the SFBA between sf writers and fandom, medieval and Renaissance re-enactors, pagans/witches/druids/magicians was so great that if you were friends with people in any one of these scenes, you could easily meet the big cheeses in the other two scenes.

replies.title: Re: James Blish and Black Easter/Day After Judgment (Devil's Day)
replies.content:
I need to correct a statement I made above. Robert Anton Wilson was acquainted with a number of witches during that period (late Sixties, early Seventies). I think Fritz Leiber was too, but I don't remember details about it, so there may have been one degree of separation.

Throughout the 1970s, NROOGD held witches' sabbats in parks and rented halls in San Francisco, Berkeley, Oakland and Marin County. Anyone who received an invitation from a NROOGD initiate or got on the mailing list could come. These rituals were regularly attended by more than one hundred people, sometimes double that number. About half were NROOGD initiates; the others included both newcomers and leaders of most of the other local pagan and magical groups, as well as some of the SCA, Renaissance Faire, science fiction and fantasy crowd (who were sometimes the same people). This was a very collegial scene; it was easy to meet anyone you wanted to talk to.

The next day, I happened to be alone in the house, and from the living room, I thought I saw a cat that looked like "Strawberry" (that was her name) scamper across the front yard several times. Curious, I opened the door, and stood in the doorway to get a better look. On 5-22-2018, I had a dream that I was Soviet soldier in WWII, about 20 years old. I was with my unit in very close combat with German soldiers. My comrades and I were very poorly organized, basically fighting individually while the Germans seemed organized, our line was falling back, and my comrades were falling dead about me; we were getting slaughtered, and inflicting very few casualties in return. I got several shots off myself, but I don’t think I hit any of the enemy. I was frankly inept as a soldier, as were my comrades, to say nothing of our commanders, who didn’t seem to be on the scene. There was at least one German soldier with a flame-thrower, and it devastated us. I scampered laterally away to evade the flames, but got burned on might right side at least, I recall falling down as the dream ended, and I’m sure that that represented my death. The dream had a sense of reality that seemed unlike a regular dream, as it not have any non-logical/non-plausible happenings or discontinuities. It made me think, for the first time, that reincarnation might be real.

Nothing in the dream seemed to locate it in time or space precisely. So I looked up some chronology of WWII see if anything was going on May 22 in any of the war years. Well, it turns out that date falls in the middle of the 2nd Battle of Kharkov, which lasted from May 12 to 28th, 1942. The Wikipedia entry, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Kharkov, seems entirely consistent with my dream, and in fact it rather startled me, so much so that I now, rightly or wrongly, identify this as the battle I was in. I don’t know if the Germans were using flame throwers in combat that early, though the battle was with the German 6th Army, and they went on to take (and lose) Stalingrad; I understand they did use flamethrowers in the Battle of Stalingrad.

So first question: If this is indeed a past life memory, what do I do with it? What might be its implications? Is this this origin of my Russophilia, or is it more likely that my dream is the result of my Russophilia?

My second question relates to my older daughter, who is now 15. I was recently telling her the history of my late father, who flew in the US Army Air Corps in Europe in WWII. My father died long before my daughter was born, but she has always felt an interest and special closeness to him, and has long been obsessed with WWII. She keeps his picture in her bedroom; of him in his uniform as a pilot in the war. It is the only picture of any relative that she keeps. Anyway, when I was recounting his shootdown over Hamburg during a bombing raid, and his parachuting out, as his navigator went down with the plane, my daughter was gobsmacked; she said “that’s it, that’s it!!!”, and she the recounted a series of recurring nightmares she had at age 4, in which she was in the sky falling in terror and anguish. She was convinced that her dream was a memory of my father getting shot down, and loosing his navigator. She even asked me his (the navigators’s) name!!, the exact question I asked my dad when he told me the story. I told her of course.

This may be a tidbit of no significance: My daughter has a startling, even eerie, physical resemblance to my dad. Even her schoolmates and teachers commented on it for veterans day when the kids all brought in photos of their veteran relatives to share. If my dad and daughter could be placed in the same place and time at the same age, they would appear to be well-matched fraternal twins.

So with my daughter, I’m trying to interpret was any of this might mean. She certainly has issues with anxiety and defiance, she and I have difficult father-daughter issues. I don't feel that she is my father reincarnated, but I do wonder it there is some inherited or other metaphysical transference of memory going on. I don’t know how to process this or what to do with it. I can’t help but wonder it if there is some connection with my own dream.

Lastly, ALL of my wife’s grandparents’ relatives in Europe, i.e. Russia and Poland, were exterminated in the Holocaust; they were Jewish of course.

What would you do with this history if it were yours?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's entirely possible that you remembered your last death in that dream, and that your daughter is your reincarnated father. Such things happen tolerably often. I'd take it as an interesting possibility and see what else surfaces. In the meantime, explore the possibility that your problems with your daughter are due to the fact that she dimly remembers being your dad, and therefore is having trouble accepting your authority over her when part of her thinks she should have authority over you. If the two of you can talk about that possibility, it might make things go more easily between the two of you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for this. I will try to assimilate it. To even think about it raises all sorts of scenarios and possible unintended/unforeseen consequences, and I need to reflect long and hard before I say/do anything. Gads, I need to figure out why I'm so resistant to divination.
